note_data.title: Diffusion, osmosis and active transport
keyPoints: **Diffusion** is the net movement of particles from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration down a concentration gradient, as a result of their random movement
**Osmosis** is the net movement of water molecules from a region of higher water potential to a region of lower water potential through a partially permeable membrane
**Active transport** is the movement of particles through a cell membrane from a region of lower concentration to a region of higher concentration using energy from respiration
Rate of diffusion increases with: higher temperature, larger concentration gradient, larger surface area, and shorter diffusion distance
**Partially permeable membranes** allow some substances to pass through but not others - water molecules can pass through but larger solute molecules cannot
In **isotonic** solutions, water potential inside and outside the cell is equal so no net movement of water occurs
In **hypotonic** solutions, cells gain water by osmosis and may burst (animal cells) or become turgid (plant cells)
In **hypertonic** solutions, cells lose water by osmosis and become crenated (animal cells) or plasmolysed (plant cells)
Active transport requires **carrier proteins** and **ATP energy** from respiration to move substances against their concentration gradient

definitions.definition: The net movement of particles from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration down a concentration gradient, as a result of their random movement
The net movement of water molecules from a region of higher water potential to a region of lower water potential through a partially permeable membrane
The movement of particles through a cell membrane from a region of lower concentration to a region of higher concentration using energy from respiration
The tendency of water molecules to move from one place to another
The difference in concentration between two regions

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
State similarities and differences between two processes
Give observable features or steps in a process

modelAnswer.question: Explain why glucose can be absorbed from the small intestine even when its concentration is higher inside the intestine cells than in the blood. (3 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: Glucose is absorbed by active transport. This process can move glucose from a region of lower concentration (blood) to a region of higher concentration (intestine cells). Active transport uses energy from respiration to work against the concentration gradient.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Glucose absorbed by active transport
Mark 2: Movement against concentration gradient/from low to high concentration

commonMistakes: Never say 'particles want to move' — always say 'particles move down their concentration gradient as a result of random movement'
Students often confuse osmosis with diffusion — osmosis is specifically for water molecules through a partially permeable membrane
Don't say 'energy is needed for osmosis' — osmosis is a passive process, only active transport needs energy
Never write 'high concentration to low concentration' — always specify 'region of higher concentration to region of lower concentration'
Students forget that active transport works AGAINST the concentration gradient, not down it
examinerTips: This topic appears in almost every paper - learn the exact definitions by heart
Always include 'partially permeable membrane' when defining osmosis
Draw arrows on diagrams to show direction of movement for full marks
Remember to link active transport to respiration and ATP energy
Use the term 'net movement' in definitions, not just 'movement'
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Cell membrane showing diffusion (small molecules passing through), osmosis (water molecules through membrane), and active transport (carrier proteins using ATP energy to move particles against gradient)
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — they asked students to identify which processes require ATP from a list including phagocytosis, active transport, and facilitated diffusion
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — students had to select features required for efficient diffusion from multiple choice options
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — candidates were asked to compare facilitated diffusion and active transport by stating one similarity and two differences
This topic was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q2 (3 marks) — students had to explain why sodium ions cannot cross phospholipid bilayers by simple diffusion

This topic appeared in M/J 2022 P3 Q1 (6 marks) — practical question asking students to analyze data on diffusion rates and evaluate whether results support the hypothesis that diffusion rate decreases over time

workedExample.question: Compare facilitated diffusion and active transport by stating one way in which they are similar and two ways in which facilitated diffusion is different from active transport. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify what both processes have in common - both involve membrane proteins for transport across cell membranes
Step 2: Think about energy requirements - facilitated diffusion is passive (no energy), active transport requires ATP energy
Step 3: Consider concentration gradients - facilitated diffusion moves down gradients, active transport can move against gradients
Step 4: Structure answer clearly with one similarity followed by two differences
workedExample.answer: Similarity: Both facilitated diffusion and active transport involve transport proteins/carrier proteins in the cell membrane. Difference 1: Facilitated diffusion moves substances down their concentration gradient whereas active transport moves substances against their concentration gradient. Difference 2: Facilitated diffusion does not require ATP/energy whereas active transport requires ATP/energy.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct similarity (transport proteins/membrane proteins). 1 mark each for two valid differences: concentration gradient direction and energy requirement. Must be clearly stated as differences.

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused active transport with facilitated diffusion' — always mention ATP requirement and concentration gradient direction when distinguishing transport processes

Examiner Report 2024: 'Students often forgot that osmosis only refers to water movement' — never describe movement of solutes as osmosis, it specifically refers to water through partially permeable membranes

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates frequently failed to link membrane structure to transport limitations' — always explain that charged particles cannot pass through hydrophobic phospholipid tails when discussing selective permeability
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a cell membrane cross-section showing phospholipid bilayer with embedded transport proteins. Include: phospholipid molecules with hydrophilic heads (circles) and hydrophobic tails (wavy lines), carrier proteins spanning the membrane, channel proteins, and arrows showing different transport directions. Label concentration gradients (high to low for passive transport, low to high for active transport) and indicate ATP usage for active transport.
note_data.quickRecap: Three main transport processes: simple diffusion (directly through membrane, no energy), facilitated diffusion (through proteins, no energy, down gradient), and active transport (through proteins, requires ATP, against gradient). Osmosis is specifically water movement through partially permeable membranes. Charged particles need protein channels because they cannot pass through hydrophobic membrane core.
